applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban neighborhood visual diversity is quantified from large collections of street-view images sampled along neighborhood street networks (e.g., points every ~20 m). AI-based image segmentation extracts semantic categories (e.g., road, building, green, terrain, sky), and multiple diversity indices (e.g., entropy, Simpson, McIntosh, Gini; plus a Horizontal View Index) are computed to relate visual diversity/inequality to neighborhood socio-demographics (e.g., population, density, income, home prices) and crime metrics using correlation analysis. Results are intended for comparing neighborhoods and understanding built/natural environment patterns; correlations do not imply causality.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational only; authors note no causal inference

source.citation: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6(1), 315–337.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Metropolitan housing market context in Busan, South Korea. Street-level greenness was measured from Google Street View images (green index based on pixel classification) and spatially interpolated/assigned to housing units by averaging the nearest 20/50/100 images to address non-uniform image coverage. Hedonic (OLS) price models controlled for property characteristics, environmental amenities (e.g., distance to green space and water), built environment (e.g., distance to subway), demographics, and seasonality. Findings reflect relationships between visual street greenness, proximity to green space, and housing prices in a dense urban residential setting; interpretation may depend on local context where street greenery can correlate with older neighborhoods or road buffers.
applicability.spatial_context: Busan, South Korea; Busan; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Google Street View; uneven spatial/temporal coverage
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: An, S., Jang, H., Kim, H., Song, Y., & Ahn, K. (2023). Assessment of street-level greenness and its association with housing prices in a metropolitan area. Scientific Reports, 13(1).

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Outdoor pedestrian walking experiment in a high-density Hong Kong street environment along predetermined routes; participants wore ambulatory sensors (skin conductance) and GPS while street-view/isovist-based visual exposure factors were linked to locations to model physiological stress with spatial machine learning.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; experimental design; machine learning modelling
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Cai, M., Xiang, L., & Ng, E. (2023). How does the visual environment influence pedestrian physiological stress? Evidence from high-density cities using ambulatory technology and spatial machine learning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 96, 104695.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Taipei City, Taiwan, using Google Street View (GSV) road-network sampling at 30 m intervals (2018–2022) to compute Green View Index (GVI 2.0) and relate it to grid-based monthly average temperature and temperature difference. Applicable to subtropical, high-density urban areas where street-level greenery (street trees and vertical greening) may not be well captured by satellite NDVI; analysis integrates GSV-derived indicators (GVI, sky view factor, building view factor), satellite NDVI (MODIS 250 m; Landsat 8 30 m), land use, traffic volume, altitude, and building area, modeled with spatial methods (e.g., geographically weighted regression).
applicability.spatial_context: Taipei City, Taiwan; Taipei City; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Chan, T.-C., Lee, P.-H., Lee, Y.-T., & Tang, J.-H. (2024). Exploring the spatial association between the distribution of temperature and urban morphology with green view index. PLOS ONE, 19(5), e0301921.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study area is Shenzhen, China (dense subtropical city). Outcome is dockless shared bicycle departure volume density (rides/ha) over two days (~902,000 rides). Key exposure is street greening measured at eye level (Green View Index from street-view images) and overhead greenness (Green Coverage Index from SPOT-5 imagery). Models control for bicycle infrastructure (available bicycle density, greenway density), land use/building environment (floor area ratio, building density, land-use entropy, POI density), and traffic environment (road density, transit station density, intersection density). Findings indicate eye-level greening (Green View Index) is positively associated with shared bicycle use across multiple time periods, while overhead greenness (Green Coverage Index) shows weak/non-significant associations.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Chen, J., Zhou, C., & Li, F. (2020). Quantifying the green view indicator for assessing urban greening quality: An analysis based on Internet-crawling street view data. Ecological Indicators, 113, 106192.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban road greenery assessment in downtown Changsha (within the Second Ring). Road network from OpenStreetMap; atypical roads (residential roads, paths, footways, links) excluded. Sampling points generated along roads in ArcGIS with a minimum spacing of 200 m, yielding 16,688 points. Baidu Street View Images (fish-eye 360-degree) collected at sampling points. Semantic segmentation performed using a deep learning FCN trained on the ADE_20K dataset to extract tree/grass/vegetation pixel shares; GVI computed as the aggregated greenery proportion visible in SVIs and mapped/compared across districts and streets.
applicability.spatial_context: Second Ring; downtown; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 200 m; Baidu Street View; FCN segmentation; ADE20K training dataset
applicability.causal_limitations: model-based prediction (not causal)

source.citation: Chen, Y., Zhang, Q., Deng, Z., Fan, X., Xu, Z., Kang, X., Pan, K., & Guo, Z. (2022). Research on Green View Index of Urban Roads Based on Street View Image Recognition: A Case Study of Changsha Downtown Areas. Sustainability, 14(23), 16063.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional image audit of 800 public open spaces (parks, gardens, plazas, playgrounds, sports fields, waterfront promenades) in Hong Kong (525 POS, 3,368 panoramas) and Singapore (275 POS, 7,708 panoramas) via Google Street View (4-direction, ca. 2022), GIS (URA, OSM) and Landsat 8. 24 indicators in six dimensions, computed with ResNet-50/segmentation; subjective scores from paired-image Amazon MTurk ratings modelled by Random Forest. Predictive/correlational, no causal inference.
applicability.study_scope: Multi-city or multi-site study; survey-based; machine learning modelling
applicability.spatial_context: Hong Kong and Singapore; Hong Kong
applicability.causal_limitations: model-based prediction (not causal)

source.citation: Chen, S., & Biljecki, F. (2023). Automatic assessment of public open spaces using street view imagery. Cities, 137, 104329.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study evaluates implied urban nature vitality (IUNV) in San Francisco by combining census socio-demographic variables (CDC Social Vulnerability Index / ACS), street view image features extracted via computer vision (including street-adjacent greenery such as trees and small green areas), and geotagged Flickr photos clustered/labelled to represent urban nature-related activity and perceptions. Analysis is conducted at spatial units within the city and relates IUNV to accessibility to urban green space (e.g., within ~1000 m walking distance), land use entropy, road density, and street-level features such as trees and sculptures.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Chen, M., Cai, Y., Guo, S., Sun, R., Song, Y., & Shen, X. (2024). Evaluating implied urban nature vitality in San Francisco: An interdisciplinary approach combining census data, street view images, and social media analysis. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 95, 128289.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study uses Tencent Street View panoramic images sampled along streets in Nanjing, China, and validates computed visual perception indices (e.g., salient region saturation, visual entropy, green view index, sky opening index) against questionnaire ratings from 100 volunteers viewing the panoramic images.
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Cheng, L., Chu, S., Zong, W., Li, S., Wu, J., & Li, M. (2017). Use of Tencent Street View Imagery for Visual Perception of Streets. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 6(9), 265.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Rural-to-urban migrants living in Wuhan, China; analysis compares inner-city versus inner-suburb neighborhood contexts and links urban green space quantity/quality (e.g., park density, park area ratio, street-level Green View Index, satisfaction, social media ratings, greenspace disorder, presence of recreational plaza-type parks) with mental health (GHQ-12) and perceived everyday discrimination using multilevel structural equation modeling.
applicability.spatial_context: Wuhan, China; Wuhan; inner-city; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Cheng, H., Li, Z., Gou, F., Wang, Z., & Zhai, W. (2024). Urban green space, perceived everyday discrimination and mental health among rural-to-urban migrants: A multilevel analysis in Wuhan, China. BMC Public Health, 24(1).

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level outdoor thermal comfort study in Taichung City, Taiwan, comparing hot season (June–September) and cool season (October–March). Sky view factor (SVF) and greenery view index (GVI) were quantified from street imagery (fisheye photos and Google Street View panoramas) using deep learning semantic segmentation (ADE20K; DeepLabV3 ResNeSt-269) and related to objective thermal comfort (PET) and subjective thermal sensation votes (TSV) for pedestrians.
applicability.spatial_context: Taichung City, Taiwan; Taichung City; street-network level
applicability.temporal_scope: summer season; spring season; autumn season
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Google Street View; field-collected imagery; DeepLabV3+ segmentation; ADE20K training dataset
applicability.causal_limitations: model-based prediction (not causal)

source.citation: Chiang, Y.-C., Liu, H.-H., Li, D., & Ho, L.-C. (2023). Quantification through deep learning of sky view factor and greenery on urban streets during hot and cool seasons. Landscape and Urban Planning, 232, 104679.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional correlation/mediation study, central Wuhan, China (8 districts, ~11M residents, 2019). 24,860 points 100 m apart on OSM main roads yielded 99,440 Tencent Street View images. FCN-8s/ADE-20K segmentation produced 7 indicators (greenness, openness, blueness, enclosure, walkability, imageability, traffic flow); 50 volunteers (20-50) rated 600 images each on 6 perceptions via adversarial Random Forest. Multiple linear regression + mediation; associational only; main-roads only.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design; image-based ratings may differ from in-situ perception

source.citation: Dai, L., Zheng, C., Dong, Z., Yao, Y., Wang, R., Zhang, X., Ren, S., Zhang, J., Song, X., & Guan, Q. (2021). Analyzing the correlation between visual space and residents' psychology in Wuhan, China using street-view images and deep-learning technique. City and Environment Interactions, 11, 100069.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional street-greenery audit of Beijing's Sixth Ring Road area (~2,267 km^2, ~21.7M residents). Beijing road vectors (Navigator Co., Q1 2013) simplified to centre-lines; 55,539 sites spaced 100 m on motorways, expressways, major/minor roads. A crawler pulled 333,234 Tencent Street View pictures (50 m radius; pitches -20/0/+20; six headings). HSI-colour segmentation gave Green View Index per site, aggregated to road and district level. Observational only; slope/illumination uncontrolled.
applicability.spatial_context: the Sixth Ring Road area of Beijing, China; Beijing; Sixth Ring Road
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 100 m; Tencent Street View
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Dong, R., Zhang, Y., & Zhao, J. (2018). How Green Are the Streets Within the Sixth Ring Road of Beijing? An Analysis Based on Tencent Street View Pictures and the Green View Index. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 15(7), 1367.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level sky openness (sky view factor, SVF) estimated from Google Street View panoramic images sampled every 100 m along main roads in the Hakata Station area, Fukuoka, Japan. Images should be from spring/summer to avoid leaf-off conditions affecting SVF. SVF computed as the proportion of pixels classified as sky after semantic segmentation. Walkability measured via a modified Walk Score using locally adapted POI categories/weights; association tested using Pearson correlation across 334 sampling points.
applicability.spatial_context: Google Street View panoramic images sampled every 100 m along main roads in the Hakata Station area, Fukuoka, Japan; Hakata Station area; street-network level
applicability.temporal_scope: summer season; spring season; note: leaf-off conditions may affect results
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 100 m; Google Street View
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Fang, Q., Inoue, T., ZHIQIU, X., & Qiang, L. (2022). Street Sky Openness Estimation Based on Deep Learning——Case Study of Hakata Station Area, Fukuoka. Proceedings of the 58th ISOCARP World Planning Congress.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Center area of Shenzhen, China. Study examines spatial associations between urban greenness (eye-level greenness from street-view images via deep-learning segmentation, and overhead greenness via NDVI from Landsat 8) and dockless bike-sharing usage. Usage is aggregated within Thiessen-polygon buffers and analyzed separately for weekdays, weekends, and national holidays using geographically weighted regression (GWR) to capture spatially varying relationships; built-environment covariates include road/intersection, transit, building, land-use, and population-related densities.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Gao, F., Li, S., Tan, Z., Zhang, X., Lai, Z., & Tan, Z. (2021). How Is Urban Greenness Spatially Associated with Dockless Bike Sharing Usage on Weekdays, Weekends, and Holidays? ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 10(4), 238.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street spatial quality assessment using Baidu Street View Images within the Second Ring Road of Changsha, China. 360° street view images (1024×512) captured in August 2019 were semantically segmented with an FCN model (ADE_20K) to extract pixel proportions of streetscape elements. Street space quality was evaluated via six indicators: sky visibility index (SVI), green visual index (GVI), interface enclosure index (IEI), public–facility convenience index (PCI), traffic recognition (TR), and motorization degree (MD), with spatial visualization/analysis by administrative region using ArcGIS.
applicability.spatial_context: the Second Ring Road of Changsha, China; Changsha; Second Ring Road
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Baidu Street View; FCN segmentation; ADE20K training dataset
applicability.causal_limitations: descriptive/mapping study

source.citation: Gao, L., Xiang, X., Chen, W., Nong, R., Zhang, Q., Chen, X., & Chen, Y. (2024). Research on Urban Street Spatial Quality Based on Street View Image Segmentation. Sustainability, 16(16), 7184.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Perceived street-scene complexity modelling using manually selected street-view panoramic images from multiple Chinese cities (Shanghai, with transfer tests to Hangzhou and Xi'an). Panoramas were screened to exclude unqualified images (over/underexposed or with excessive interferers) and excluded elevated roads, tunnels, and indoor parking lots. Scenes included residential, commercial, and office areas. Human participants (a deliberately homogeneous group, mostly ages 20–29) rated each panorama on a 1–5 complexity scale; average scores across participants were used as ground truth. Features integrated visual, structural (e.g., road network/branches/nodes), and semantic (e.g., POI counts and spatial patterns) information; Random Forest regression achieved best performance when using all feature dimensions.
applicability.study_scope: Multi-city or multi-site study; regression modelling; machine learning modelling
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: image quality sensitive to lighting/weather; excluded tunnels/viaducts/elevated roads
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Guan, F., Fang, Z., Wang, L., Zhang, X., Zhong, H., & Huang, H. (2022). Modelling people's perceived scene complexity of real-world environments using street-view panoramas and open geodata. ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing, 186, 315–331.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Case study in Wuhan, China (central urban areas) assessing inequities in street-level green visibility using time-series Baidu Street View images. Residential locations were aggregated to 250 m hexagonal grid centroids, and green visibility (GVI) was calculated from street-view sampling points within a 1000 m buffer around each residential aggregation point to represent daily living space access.
applicability.spatial_context: Wuhan, China; Wuhan; central urban area; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: Causal limitations not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Guo, X., Liu, C., Bi, S., & Tang, Y. (2025). Identification of Inequities in Green Visibility and Ways to Increase Greenery in Neighborhoods: A Case Study of Wuhan, China. Applied Sciences, 15(2), 742.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street environments in major districts of Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China, assessed during full-leaf seasons (spring, summer, fall) using panoramic street-view images. Street greening quality is quantified via semantic segmentation (e.g., grass/flowers/shrubs/trees) and indicators such as Plant Color Richness, Plant Level Diversity, Green View Index, and Tree–Sky View Factor/line of sight, and linked to emotional perception scores (pleasure, relaxation, boredom, anxiety/worry) modeled with random forest and interpreted with SHAP.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; machine learning modelling
applicability.spatial_context: major districts of Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China; Hangzhou
applicability.temporal_scope: summer season; spring season; autumn season; vegetation leaf-on period
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Hao, N., Li, X., Han, D., & Nie, W. (2024). Quantifying the Impact of Street Greening during Full-Leaf Seasons on Emotional Perception: Guidelines for Resident Well-Being. Forests, 15(1), 119.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Beijing, China, examining associations between objectively measured street-view green and blue space exposure around residential neighborhoods and geriatric depressive symptoms (GDS-15). Exposure was derived from Tencent street view images (2012) segmented using a fully convolutional neural network; analyses used multilevel linear models and propensity score matching with individual covariates (e.g., age, gender, education, hukou, marital status).
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; machine learning modelling
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design; addressed residential self-selection

source.citation: Helbich, M., Yao, Y., Liu, Y., Zhang, J., Liu, P., & Wang, R. (2019). Using deep learning to examine street view green and blue spaces and their associations with geriatric depression in Beijing, China. Environment International, 126, 107–117.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study focuses on the high-density urban area within Chengdu's Third Ring Road (Chengdu, China). It coordinates construction of an urban ecological network and a street green space network using Baidu street-view imagery to compute Green View Index (GVI) and ArcGIS-based spatial analyses (e.g., Getis-Ord Gi* hot/cold spots; bivariate spatial autocorrelation with ecological resistance surfaces). Applicable to dense inner-city street networks where street-view coverage is available and where ecological network connectivity and street-level greening are jointly assessed.
applicability.spatial_context: Chengdu; Chengdu's Third Ring Road; inner-city; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Hou, S., Yu, Y., Jung, T., & Han, X. (2025). How to Coordinate Urban Ecological Networks and Street Green Space Construction? Insights from a Multi-Scale Perspective. Land, 14(1), 26.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Pilot case study in Osaka City, Japan, evaluating urban greening by integrating horizontal green coverage ratio (GCR) from Sentinel-2 NDVI (satellite imagery dated 2021-10-02) and vertical green view index (GVI) from Google Street View images (mainly captured in 2019) using semantic segmentation (e.g., PSPNet). Analysis unit is elementary school administrative districts (generated July 2022) to represent residents' daily experience; results are mapped and analyzed in ArcGIS. Method is intended to be transferable to other cities, with data sources/tools varying by availability.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Hu, A., Yabuki, N., Fukuda, T., Kaga, H., Takeda, S., & Matsuo, K. (2023). Harnessing multiple data sources and emerging technologies for comprehensive urban green space evaluation. Cities, 143, 104562.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Huangpu District, Shanghai, China – a representative high-density, high-utilisation urban area with population density exceeding 300 people per hectare. Vehicle-mounted mobile monitoring experiments collected PM2.5 and PM10 spatial distribution data along three typical streets. Nine urban morphology indicators were computed using GIS, including sky view factor (SVF), green view index (GVI), building height, floor area ratio, building coverage ratio, building shape factor, and standard deviation of building height. Street-view imagery was used to derive SVF and GVI via semantic segmentation. Multivariate statistical analysis examined correlations between pollutant concentrations and morphological indicators, and machine learning regression models (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost) were trained to predict pollutant levels from urban morphology features.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; experimental design; regression modelling; machine learning modelling
applicability.spatial_context: Huangpu District, Shanghai, China; Huangpu District
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Huang, C., Hu, T., Duan, Y., Li, Q., Chen, N., Wang, Q., Zhou, M., & Rao, P. (2022). Effect of urban morphology on air pollution distribution in high-density urban blocks based on mobile monitoring and machine learning. Building and Environment, 219, 109173.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street spatial quality measurement using street view images in the central urban area of Shenyang (central city/central district). Indicators are derived from semantic segmentation of street view imagery (e.g., green visual index, street width/openness, enclosure, motorization, spatial feasibility, public-facility convenience, vehicle interference, interface transparency) and correlated with subjective perception scores across a large street sample (3,262 streets). Applicable to evaluating and mapping street quality patterns and informing differentiated street improvement strategies in dense urban street networks.
applicability.spatial_context: Shenyang; central urban area; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Jin, K., Yang, T., & Guo, W. (2023). Urban Street Quality Measurement Methods Combining Street View Images. 2023 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Intelligent Information Processing (AIIIP), 29–38.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Residents in central Guangzhou, China, in highly urbanized neighborhoods. Street-view greenery was measured along the road network using Baidu Street View images captured every 50 m in four directions (0/90/180/270 degrees) and extracted via deep-learning semantic segmentation (FCN-8s). Outcome is residents' fear of neighborhood crime; analyses control for individual and neighborhood characteristics and test mediation via perceived physical/social incivilities and social integration.
applicability.spatial_context: central Guangzhou, China; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 50 m; Baidu Street View; FCN segmentation
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Jing, F., Liu, L., Zhou, S., Song, J., Wang, L., Zhou, H., Wang, Y., & Ma, R. (2021). Assessing the Impact of Street-View Greenery on Fear of Neighborhood Crime in Guangzhou, China. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 18(1), 311.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Comparative study of 2D vs 3D urban green spaces in central Shenzhen (Nanshan, Futian, Luohu districts), China, summer 2023. Baidu Street View images along the urban road network were processed by a CNN for semantic segmentation to compute Green View Index (GVI), then compared with satellite-derived NDVI via Pearson correlation and a four-quadrant typology, with building height, density and elevated transport discussed as drivers.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 100 m; Baidu Street View; DeepLabV3+ segmentation
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Jing, X., Li, Z., Chen, H., & Zhang, C. (2024). "Is What We See Always Real?" A Comparative Study of Two-Dimensional and Three-Dimensional Urban Green Spaces: The Case of Shenzhen's Central District. Forests, 15(6), 983.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: This paper is a narrative review of public health studies that sense and audit urban physical/built environments using street view imagery (e.g., Google/Baidu/Tencent Street View). It discusses methods to represent built-environment elements and scenes from eye-level street imagery and summarizes associations with health outcomes (e.g., walking behaviors, depression/anxiety, restorative effects). The scope is global across many cities/countries rather than a single study site, so no single climate or LCZ applies.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Baidu Street View; Tencent Street View
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design; review paper; findings synthesised from multiple sources

source.citation: Kang, Y., Zhang, F., Gao, S., Lin, H., & Liu, Y. (2020). A review of urban physical environment sensing using street view imagery in public health studies. Annals of GIS, 26(3), 261–275.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban streetscape study in Colombo, Sri Lanka (tropical context). Visual perception assessed using 48 Google Street View Images (SVIs) of streets within a 1 km radius from Colpetty Junction; outcomes include perceived safety, comfort/preference, and perceived complexity, compared against objective visual complexity measures (Shannon Diversity Index and Fractal Dimension).
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Kawshalya, L. W. G., Weerasinghe, U. G. D., & Chandrasekara, D. P. (2022). The impact of visual complexity on perceived safety and comfort of the users: A study on urban streetscape of Sri Lanka. PLOS ONE, 17(8), e0272074.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Seoul, Republic of Korea. Neighborhood street greenery exposure was measured using Google Street View images sampled at 50 m intervals and deep-learning semantic segmentation to compute Green View Index (GVI). Built-environment variables (including GVI, park area, street trees, intersections, land-use mix, traffic safety, distance to transportation, water area) were calculated within a 500 m buffer around respondents' residences. Outcomes were self-reported walking time near home in the last week, separated into utilitarian and leisure walking, with controls for individual/household factors and self-selection variables (walking will and residential location choice).
applicability.spatial_context: Seoul, Republic of Korea; Seoul; neighbourhood scale
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias); addressed residential self-selection

source.citation: Ki, D., & Lee, S. (2021). Analyzing the effects of Green View Index of neighborhood streets on walking time using Google Street View and deep learning. Landscape and Urban Planning, 205, 103920.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Seoul, Republic of Korea. Neighborhood street greenery exposure was measured using Google Street View images sampled at 50 m intervals and deep-learning semantic segmentation to compute Green View Index (GVI). Built-environment variables (including GVI, park area, street trees, intersections, land-use mix, traffic safety, distance to transportation, water area) were calculated within a 500 m buffer around respondents' residences. Outcomes were self-reported walking time near home in the last week, separated into utilitarian and leisure walking, with controls for individual/household factors and self-selection variables (walking will and residential location choice).
applicability.spatial_context: Seoul, Republic of Korea; Seoul; neighbourhood scale
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias); addressed residential self-selection

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in the Portland, Oregon Metropolitan Statistical Area (MSA), using georeferenced Google Street View (GSV) images to measure visible greenness (Green View Index) and compare/augment bird's-eye greenspace measures (e.g., NDVI, tree cover, distance to parks). Images have variable temporal coverage (years 2007–2016) and are more frequently captured in clear summer months; about 20% of sampled images were taken in other seasons. Potential biases include uneven spatial/temporal GSV coverage (especially rural areas) and pixel-based classification errors that may misclassify manmade green objects (e.g., green cars/signs) as greenspace; machine learning is suggested to improve utility.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; longitudinal/temporal analysis; machine learning modelling
applicability.temporal_scope: data period: 2007–2016; summer season; spring season
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Google Street View; uneven spatial/temporal coverage
applicability.causal_limitations: pixel classification errors possible

source.citation: Larkin, A., & Hystad, P. (2019). Evaluating street view exposure measures of visible green space for health research. Journal of Exposure Science & Environmental Epidemiology, 29(4), 447–456.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study uses Place Pulse 2.0 Google Street View image locations sampled across 56 cities in 32 countries (multiple climates). Built environment perceptions (safety, lively, beauty) are predicted using street-level visible features from segmented street view images and compared to GIS/remote sensing measures (e.g., NDVI within 250 m; other GIS variables sampled around image locations). Applicable to urban street environments where street view imagery and GIS/remote sensing covariates are available; results emphasize that street-level visible features explain more variance in perceptions than GIS/remote sensing alone.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Larkin, A., Gu, X., Chen, L., & Hystad, P. (2021). Predicting perceptions of the built environment using GIS, satellite and street view image approaches. Landscape and Urban Planning, 216, 104257.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Narrative review of deep learning-based semantic segmentation methods and datasets for measuring urban greenness from both plan-view aerial imagery (satellite/UAV/airplane) and street-view imagery (e.g., GVI). Includes discussion of datasets such as ISPRS Potsdam/Vaihingen (plan-view) and Cityscapes/ADE20K (street-view/scene parsing), and an example application measuring urban greenness across 31 major cities in China using a high-resolution urban green space dataset (UGSet) from Gaofen-2 satellite imagery. Applicable to general urban contexts rather than a single site.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: ADE20K training dataset; Cityscapes training dataset
applicability.causal_limitations: review paper; findings synthesised from multiple sources

source.citation: Lee, D.H. et al. (2024). A Review on Recent Deep Learning-Based Semantic Segmentation for Urban Greenness Measurement. Sensors, 24, 2245.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Comparative study of street-quality perceptions in Shanghai and Chengdu, two Chinese megacities. Baidu Street View Images sampled at 50 m intervals via Baidu Maps API were processed with a TensorFlow semantic-segmentation network for street elements. Space syntax (ArcGIS 10.4, 500 m radius) measured pedestrian accessibility; a human-machine adversarial model trained on volunteer ratings yielded six perception indicators, and multiple linear regression linked perceptions to elements.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: FCN segmentation; image quality sensitive to lighting/weather
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Lei, Y.; Zhou, H.; Xue, L.; Yuan, L.; Liu, Y.; Wang, M.; Wang, C. (2024). Evaluating and Comparing Human Perceptions of Streets in Two Megacities by Integrating Street-View Images, Pedestrians' Surveys, and Spatial Syntax. Buildings, 14(6), 1847.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level urban greenery assessment using Google Street View (GSV) images and a modified Green View Index (GVI). Study uses randomly generated sample sites along the road network and computes visible greenness from 360° horizontal coverage (six directions) and multiple vertical view angles (three pitches), totaling 18 GSV images per site. Intended to represent greenness pedestrians can see; vegetation is extracted from RGB imagery via an automatic unsupervised classification approach, with noted challenges from shadows and confusion with manmade green objects due to lack of near-infrared bands.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Google Street View; image quality sensitive to lighting/weather
applicability.causal_limitations: pixel classification errors possible

source.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level urban landscape mapping using Google Street View panoramas in Boston, Massachusetts. The study quantifies streetscape characteristics including street greenery (modified Green View Index) and street canyon openness (Sky View Factor) from publicly accessible GSV imagery, using automated image processing and geometric transformation to generate fisheye views for SVF estimation. Applicable to city-scale street environments, including contrasts between downtown areas with lower greenery and residential areas with higher greenery.
applicability.causal_limitations: descriptive/mapping study

source.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2017). Mapping Urban Landscapes Along Streets Using Google Street View. Lecture Notes in Geoinformation and Cartography, 341–356.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level urban landscape mapping using Google Street View panoramas in Boston, Massachusetts. The study quantifies streetscape characteristics including street greenery (modified Green View Index) and street canyon openness (Sky View Factor) from publicly accessible GSV imagery, using automated image processing and geometric transformation to generate fisheye views for SVF estimation. Applicable to city-scale street environments, including contrasts between downtown areas with lower greenery and residential areas with higher greenery.
applicability.causal_limitations: descriptive/mapping study

applicability.spatial_context: Downtown Boston, Massachusetts, USA; street-canyon level (GSV panorama sites sampled every 100 m along the street network)
applicability.raw_conditions_text: Quantification of street-tree shade provision in downtown Boston using 297 Google Street View panoramas (green/growing season) transformed into hemispherical fisheye images. SVF computed two ways: (i) GSV photographic SVF (SVF_P) capturing buildings + tree canopy obstructions via mean-shift segmentation + Otsu thresholding + ExG vegetation discrimination + geometric refinement; (ii) building-only simulated SVF (SVF_S) using a LiDAR-derived nDSM and MassGIS building footprints, 360-degree obstruction-angle search to 500 m. The difference SVF_diff = SVF_S - SVF_P estimates tree-attributable shade. Pearson correlations of SVF_diff against percentage canopy cover (PCC), average canopy height (ACH), and average building height (ABH) computed at 20/40/60/80/100 m buffers around each site (n=295).
applicability.population_scope: Not applicable (study unit is street site, not human subjects)
applicability.causal_limitations: Correlational/observational; results reflect green-season GSV imagery only and may not generalize to leaf-off seasons; sampling on street centerlines may differ from sidewalk-perceived shade; obstructions other than buildings and trees (awnings, signs, etc.) are not modeled in the simulation arm.

source.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81–91.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Roadside urban thermal environment study using street view images to quantify roadside vegetation types (grass, shrubs, trees) and relate them to land surface temperature (LST) retrieved from a Landsat-8 image (30 m resolution) acquired around 10:52 a.m. Beijing time on 2 Nov 2019. Study area is Guangzhou; analyses include hotspot analysis (Getis-Ord Gi*) and geographically weighted regression (GWR) with buffers and sampling aligned to 30 m intervals.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Li, B., Xing, H., Cao, D., Yang, G., & Zhang, H. (2022). Exploring the Effects of Roadside Vegetation on the Urban Thermal Environment Using Street View Images. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 19(3), 1272.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Pilot field study at Chufeng Park along East Lake, Wuhan, China (Köppen Cfa), March-May 2021. 54 volunteer college students used the Urbanxyz mobile PPGIS app to collect 1,560 geotagged park images and rate perceptual measures (landscape preference, place attachment, restoration). Deep-learning semantic segmentation extracted greenery and water features; OLS, quantile regression and multiscale GWR modelled psychological restoration and spatial heterogeneity.
applicability.spatial_context: Wuhan, China; single urban park (pilot study)
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Applies to rapidly urbanizing, high-density urban neighborhoods in Wuhan, China, where ischemic heart disease (IHD) hospitalization counts are analyzed at the community level (2016–2018) in relation to multiple urban green space indicators (including street-view Green View Index) and built-environment factors; effects may vary spatially and may be attenuated in compact, traffic- and heat-impacted districts with limited open space.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Li, X., Liu, Q., Liu, X., & Ren, Y. (2025). Greener is healthier: Unraveling the distinction of health impacts of urban green indicators in Wuhan, China. Sustainable Cities and Society, 125, 106318.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study area is the main island of Singapore. Visual environment patterns are derived from multi-year street view imagery aggregated to H3 hexagon research units (resolution 10) and connected via the road network as an undirected graph. Visual indicators represent inherent physical environment components (sky, vegetation, building, road) with temporary objects excluded, and perceptual attributes are also analyzed. Applicable to street-level urban visual environment characterization and its spatial distribution and temporal evolution across the city.
applicability.spatial_context: island of Singapore; Singapore; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: descriptive/mapping study

source.citation: Liang, X., Zhao, T., & Biljecki, F. (2023). Revealing spatio-temporal evolution of urban visual environments with street view imagery. Landscape and Urban Planning, 237, 104802.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional survey of 1,150 adult residents (18-70) from 23 communities across seven districts of Guangzhou, China, via multi-stage stratified PPS, June-August 2015. Access to natural outdoor environments (green and blue spaces) within a 1,000 m home buffer was measured via streetscape indices (Tencent street-view + deep learning) and satellite land-cover metrics. Subjective well-being (SWLS, PANAS) was modelled with seemingly unrelated regression plus socioeconomic-stratified analyses.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; cross-sectional design; regression modelling
applicability.spatial_context: urban residents in Guangzhou, China; Guangzhou
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design; cross-sectional (no temporal causality)

source.citation: Liu, Y., Xiao, T., Liu, Y., Yao, Y., & Wang, R. (2021). Natural outdoor environments and subjective well-being in Guangzhou, China: Comparing different measures of access. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 59, 127027.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted on main streets within the third ring area of Wuhan, China. Analysis used 15,199 valid street-view images (excluding tunnels, viaducts, and poor lighting) and Landsat 8 land surface temperature imagery (30 m resolution) with <5% cloud cover from 15 September 2018. Focus on street green space morphology and surrounding built environment indicators to model nonlinear cooling effects (SUHI/LST).
applicability.spatial_context: streets within the third ring area of Wuhan, China; Wuhan; third ring
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: image quality sensitive to lighting/weather; excluded tunnels/viaducts/elevated roads
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Liu, Z., Ma, X., Hu, L., Liu, Y., Lu, S., Chen, H., & Tan, Z. (2022). Nonlinear Cooling Effect of Street Green Space Morphology: Evidence from a Gradient Boosting Decision Tree and Explainable Machine Learning Approach. Land, 11(12), 2220.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Case study in Harbin, China, measuring legibility of public open spaces using street view images (SVIs). SVIs with major occlusions (e.g., trucks, buses, other objects) were manually removed; remaining images were classified with a DCNN and interpreted with Grad-CAM. Human validation used an online survey where adult professionals and non-professionals watched a 15-second panoramic animation of a spatial segment, then selected the matching SVI from multiple choices and clicked on image regions reflecting their decision process. Authors note potential sensitivity to season, daylight, and weather at image collection time, and possible bias from using animated panoramas and the limited sensory information in SVIs.
applicability.causal_limitations: image-based ratings may differ from in-situ perception

source.citation: Liu, W., Hu, X., Song, Z., & Yuan, X. (2023). Identifying the integrated visual characteristics of greenway landscape: A focus on human perception. Sustainable Cities and Society, 99, 104937.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Editorial overview of methods for measuring human-scale urban form and performance, including street-view image analysis. Mentions a case in the Hutong area of Beijing using Tencent Street View imagery (2012 and 2016) to assess visual quality of street space and its temporal variation; context is human-scale street environments in dense historic urban fabric.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Long, Y., & Ye, Y. (2019). Measuring human-scale urban form and its performance. Landscape and Urban Planning, 191, 103612.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Hong Kong SAR (China) public rental housing residents; eye-level street greenness exposure measured from Google Street View images using PSPNet within 400 m and 800 m circular buffers around geocoded dwelling locations; outcome focused on walking behavior from a population-level survey; cross-sectional design with reduced residential self-selection due to public housing scheme.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; cross-sectional design; survey-based
applicability.causal_limitations: cross-sectional (no temporal causality); addressed residential self-selection

source.citation: Lu, Y. (2018). The Association of Urban Greenness and Walking Behavior: Using Google Street View and Deep Learning Techniques to Estimate Residents' Exposure to Urban Greenness. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 15(8), 1576.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Mega-city context in Chengdu, China. Physical activity measured as cycling and running intensity from Strava heatmap trajectories aggregated to 250 m × 250 m grids (December 2021). Built environment assessed with human-scale streetscape indicators derived from Baidu 360° street-view semantic segmentation (green view index, sky view index, road view index) plus urban form and transport controls (e.g., FAR, land-use mix, road density, bus stations). Spatial heterogeneity analyzed using GWR to estimate locally varying associations between streetscape/built environment and cycling/running.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Luo, P., Yu, B., Li, P., & Liang, P. (2022). Spatially varying impacts of the built environment on physical activity from a human-scale view: Using street view data. Frontiers in Environmental Science, 10.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: University campus walking spaces in Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China, evaluated for perceived restorative quality using street view images and in-field questionnaires with students rating images on a 7-point Likert scale; visual features include low-level color/edge metrics and higher-level semantic/depth features extracted via pretrained deep learning models and OpenCV, with restorative quality modeled using machine learning (GBDT) against PRS-11-related ratings.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; survey-based; machine learning modelling
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Case study in Tianhe District, Guangzhou (China). Human perception of greenery and open spaces is proxied using Baidu street view images sampled every 100 m along secondary roads; greenery and open space are represented by segmented greenery pixels and sky pixels, interpolated to continuous surfaces using IDW, and related to urban poverty (modified IMD) using GWR.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional case study, Kitakyushu, Fukuoka, Japan (~491.95 km^2, 7 districts, coastal city). 2D indicators (NDVI, NDBI, FCV, SDIH) from Landsat-8 (30 m, June 2024) on a 500 m fishnet (2,630 cells). applicability.raw_conditions_text: Summer urban community street thermal comfort assessment using 1 m evaluation units. Inputs include drone-derived high-resolution DOM/DSM (DJI M300 RTK; DOM ~4.7 cm) for buildings/trees/roads, UMEP (QGIS) pixel-based shadow mapping including vegetation canopy, street-view imagery for Green View Index (GVI), and GIS/Python (ArcGIS 10.8) road width calculation at 1 m intervals for cycling and pedestrian paths. Multi-scale indicators analyzed at 10 m and 200 m scales; comfort scoring via AHP-TOPSIS to produce a greenway/street comfort evaluation map prioritizing shading protection and spatial efficiency.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study area is Shibuya, Tokyo, Japan. Sky view factor (SVF) was calculated along streets using Google Street View panoramic images and image processing, then mapped and statistically compared across land-use zoning (e.g., quasi-residential and commercial districts) and road contexts (including wide trunk roads and intersections). Validation included comparison of SVF from Google Street View with SVF derived from fisheye-lens photographs at selected points.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street-level study assessing associations between Google Street View-derived greenspace (Green View Index, plus NDVI and GVI:NDVI) and particulate air pollution (PM2.5, PM10, PM1) measured by a distributed PurpleAir sensor network. Analysis used multiple buffer radii (100 m, 250 m, 500 m, 1000 m, 2000 m) around 12 monitoring stations, with models adjusted for temperature, humidity, and traffic volumes. applicability.raw_conditions_text: Tiexi District, Shenyang, China, old industrial area under renewal at five time slices (1997, 2002, 2008, 2015, 2020). Buildings (outline/height, 6 classes) digitized from 1997 aerial (1:10,000), 2002/2008 QuickBird (0.61 m), 2015/2020 Google (1 m) imagery. BGVI via viewshed on 1 m DEM. 800 sites at 500 m yielded 14,400 Baidu Street View images (18/site); FCN segmentation (test 66.83 %, train 81.44 %) gave SVGVI. Getis-Ord Gi*; Random Forest. Observational, cross-sectional; RF predictive.
applicability.spatial_context: Street-level (eye-level) and building-level (3-D viewshed) at 800 sample sites
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Baidu Street View images (2015–2020 only); pre-2015 BGVI from digitised historical aerial imagery; FCN test accuracy 66.83 %.
applicability.causal_limitations: Observational, cross-sectional comparison at five time slices; RF associations are predictive, not causal. Authors note that the viewshed BGVI may misrepresent window-level greenery for individual buildings.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Highway greening landscape spatial quality evaluation using driving-perspective, high-definition street-view/scene images collected along the Beijing section of the Beijing-Tibet Expressway (from Madian Bridge to the Beijing municipal boundary). Quantitative indicators are extracted via deep-learning panoptic segmentation (Panoptic-DeepLab) and color analysis (K-means clustering in RGB/HSV) to characterize visible green and related landscape structure; applicable to highway corridors including plain and mountainous segments and special sections such as sound barriers, hard walls/slope protection, elevated bridges, and tunnel entrances/exits.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: DeepLabV3+ segmentation; panoptic segmentation; excluded tunnels/viaducts/elevated roads
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Qin, X., Yang, D., & Wangari, V. W. (2024). Quantitative characterization and evaluation of highway greening landscape spatial quality based on deep learning. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 107, 107559.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level perceptual quality measurement in Pudong District, Shanghai using Baidu Street View images with consistent camera settings, combined with POI data and OSM road networks. Public perceptions were collected via an online pairwise-comparison survey (23 mostly architecture-student volunteers; 3,120 valid entries) to create ranked 0–10 labels using the TrueSkill algorithm, then used as training data to predict four perceptual qualities across the district. Best suited where street view imagery is available; segmentation relied on pre-trained models and the rater sample was limited and not randomly selected.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; survey-based; pairwise comparison / ranking
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban green space landscape images evaluated for public ecosystem aesthetic perception using a PEAP framework that combines deep-learning-extracted landscape elements/features with eye-tracking indicators and perception questionnaire selections. Images were standardized (31° horizontal × 21° vertical view; 3968×2976 px) and a subset of 24 test images was selected based on cumulative selection frequencies across quality categories. Deep learning was implemented with Keras/TensorFlow (CUDA acceleration) and trained using the ADE20K annotated image dataset; indicator scores were derived from the probability of an image being selected under each landscape concept indicator.
applicability.spatial_context: urban green space sites (city unspecified in conditions)
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Qiu, Y., Pan, H., Kalantari, Z., Giusti, M., & Che, S. (2023). The natural focus: Combining deep learning and eye-tracking to understand public perceptions of urban ecosystem aesthetics. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111181.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Applies to visual evaluation of urban street landscape spatial quality using open street-view images in the main urban area of Fuzhou (Gulou, Taijiang, Cangshan, and Jin'an districts). Indicators are extracted via semantic segmentation and evaluated using ELO scoring and a random forest model, then mapped across thousands of street points.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; machine learning modelling
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Rui, Q., & Cheng, H. (2023). Quantifying the spatial quality of urban streets with open street view images: A case study of the main urban area of Fuzhou. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111204.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional study in Sendai City, Japan, examining associations between neighbourhood street greenery at different vertical viewing angles (from Google Street View images segmented with DeepLab v3+) and residents' leisure walking time. Neighbourhood defined as road-network buffer within 1000 m of residence; analysis uses multilevel mixed-effects models with individual covariates and random intercept at elementary school district level.
applicability.spatial_context: Sendai City, Japan; Sendai City; 1000 m of residence; analysis uses multilevel mixed-effects models with individual covariates and random intercept at elementary school district; neighbourhood scale
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design; cross-sectional (no temporal causality)

source.citation: Sakamoto, S., Kogure, M., Hanibuchi, T., Nakaya, N., Hozawa, A., & Nakaya, T. (2023). Effects of greenery at different heights in neighbourhood streetscapes on leisure walking: a cross-sectional study using machine learning of streetscape images in Sendai City, Japan. International Journal of Health Geographics, 22, 29.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level greenness visibility (GVI) modelling framework using open-source street view imagery (Mapillary) sampled along street networks (e.g., points every 50 m) and semantic segmentation; missing street-view locations can be supplemented with satellite-derived NDVI from Sentinel-2 (10 m) with NDVI values below 0 removed to exclude non-vegetated pixels. Method evaluates image availability/usability and compares Mapillary-based GVI with Google Street View (GSV) GVI across multiple global cities; intended for scalable citywide street-network applications rather than a single neighbourhood type.
applicability.spatial_context: citywide scale; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 50 m; Google Street View; Mapillary (crowdsourced)
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Sánchez, I. A. V., & Labib, S. M. (2024). Accessing eye-level greenness visibility from open-source street view images: A methodological development and implementation in multi-city and multi-country contexts. Sustainable Cities and Society, 103, 105262.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: City-scale assessment of perceived visual comfort on urban streets using Baidu street view images (2019) and AI-based semantic segmentation to compute street indicator percentages, followed by clustering of street types and an online questionnaire (Likert scale) where participants rate comfort of sampled street images. Conducted across four Chinese megacities (Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Shenzhen) with results mapped using GIS and kernel density analysis; ratings are image-based and collected online, so they may differ from in-person comfort in real settings.
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias); image-based ratings may differ from in-situ perception

source.citation: Shao, Y., Yin, Y., Xue, Z., & Ma, D. (2023). Assessing and Comparing the Visual Comfort of Streets across Four Chinese Megacities Using AI-Based Image Analysis and the Perceptive Evaluation Method. Land, 12(4), 834.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Visual analytics of human-scale urban form using large-scale street-view imagery and semantic segmentation (e.g., greenery, sky, building, road, vehicle). Users explore and compare areas of interest (AOIs) and streets via interactive views (AOI/Street explorers, ranking and comparison workflows). Example comparisons include Greater London (City of London as a dense center) versus Singapore, and district-level comparisons (e.g., Hong Kong). Classification is preprocessed offline; the system does not support on-demand analysis of street views queried at runtime.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Shen, Q., Zeng, W., Ye, Y., Arisona, S. M., Schubiger, S., Burkhard, R., & Qu, H. (2018). StreetVizor: Visual Exploration of Human-Scale Urban Forms Based on Street Views. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, 24(1), 1004–1013.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Residential communities in Beijing, China, analysed using Baidu Street View (BSV) images and a Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index (NVGI). The study manually delineated residential communities and their inner road networks, sampled 7,695 sites across 701 communities, quantified green view index values, and analysed their spatial heterogeneity with Moran's I and Getis-Ord Gi* hot/cold spot analysis.
applicability.spatial_context: Beijing, China; residential communities across 16 districts
applicability.population_scope: resident perspective captured via street-view imagery; 7,695 sampling sites
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Baidu Street View; residential communities and inner-road networks were manually identified.
applicability.causal_limitations: Cross-sectional spatial analysis of greenery distribution; supports descriptive spatial heterogeneity rather than causal claims.

source.citation: Song, J., Zhu, X., & Yu, X. (2023). Monitoring and mapping vegetation greenery in residential communities using street view images and a Normalized Vegetation Greenery Index: a case study in Beijing, China. Frontiers in Forests and Global Change, 6, 1071569.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Case study in Milan (Local Identity Nuclei 22, NIL Città Studi). The method analyzes street-level perceived urban greenery using street view images and semantic segmentation to compute Green View Index (GVI) and derive directional visual vectors, producing a vector field representation (Visual Greenery Field) across the street network.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Stancato, G. (2024). The Visual Greenery Field: Representing the Urban Green Visual Continuum with Street View Image Analysis. Sustainability, 16(21), 9512.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban residential communities in Dalian, a secondary hilly coastal city in northeastern China. Survey conducted July–August 2018 in urban centers with similar economic backgrounds. Focus on associations between multiple greenery measures (e.g., NDVI from 30 m satellite imagery and street-level greenness) and residents' physical activity and health outcomes, with slope/topography considered (objective slope variability and perceived hilly streets).
applicability.spatial_context: Dalian, a secondary hilly coastal city in northeastern China; Dalian; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Sun, P., Song, Y., & Lu, W. (2022). Effect of Urban Green Space in the Hilly Environment on Physical Activity and Health Outcomes: Mediation Analysis on Multiple Greenery Measures. Land, 11(5), 612.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Coastal street segments in southern Qingdao, China (about 17.15 km). Street-level greenery was quantified using street view images (GVI as the pixel proportion of visible green vegetation, via semantic segmentation such as DeepLabV3+). Visual comfort (VICO) was rated by adult participants on a 5-point scale after viewing each street view image for 20 seconds; relationships between GVI and VICO were analyzed using Pearson correlation and regression. Findings are most applicable to pedestrian eye-level perception on urban coastal streets with mixed historic, transitional, and modern districts.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Sun, D., Ji, X., Gao, W., Zhou, F., Yu, Y., Meng, Y., Yang, M., Lin, J., & Lyu, M. (2023). The Relation between Green Visual Index and Visual Comfort in Qingdao Coastal Streets. Buildings, 13(2), 457.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Zhengzhou, China, focusing on central urban districts. Streetscape perceptions (greenness, openness, enclosure, walkability, imageability) were derived from Baidu Street View images at 20,002 sampling points. Human activity density was estimated from Baidu map brightness via Baidu API using four consecutive days of data (weekdays and weekends) collected between May 2017 and February 2019. Spatial correlation between human activity density and perception scores was analyzed using entropy weighting and bivariate Moran’s I (ArcGIS and GeoDA).
applicability.temporal_scope: data period: 2017, 2019; winter season; spring season
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Tao, Y., Wang, Y., Wang, X., Tian, G., & Zhang, S. (2022). Measuring the Correlation between Human Activity Density and Streetscape Perceptions: An Analysis Based on Baidu Street View Images in Zhengzhou, China. Land, 11(3), 400.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street greenery assessment in Nanjing, China (Gulou, Xuanwu, Jianye, Qinhuai, and northern Yuhuatai). Uses street-level Tencent Street View images (six directions per site) with semantic segmentation (PSPNet) to compute Green View Index (GVI), plus Landsat 8 satellite imagery (30 m, October 2013) to compute NDVI, and a vegetation structure diversity indicator (VSD) to capture tree–shrub–herb layering. Road network sourced from OpenStreetMap with topological correction; urban functional zones from a 500 m × 500 m grid dataset.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Tong, M., She, J., Tan, J., Li, M., Ge, R., & Gao, Y. (2020). Evaluating Street Greenery by Multiple Indicators Using Street-Level Imagery and Satellite Images: A Case Study in Nanjing, China. Forests, 11(12), 1347.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street greenery assessment in Nanjing, China (Gulou, Xuanwu, Jianye, Qinhuai, and northern Yuhuatai). applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Nanjing, China (Nanjing University Gulou campus and surrounding streets). Uses 360-degree Baidu Street View panoramas re-projected to fisheye images and semantic segmentation to compute street view factors (SVF, TVF, BVF) across different vertical viewing angle ranges; applicable to campus-adjacent urban streets with mature street trees and building enclosure where vertical viewing angle selection affects visual indices.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Tong, Z., & Gong, C. (2022). Integrating Characteristics of Vertical Viewing Angle into Quantitative Analysis of Street View Photos. 2022 29th International Conference on Geoinformatics, 1–6.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Street-level perceived greenery study conducted at multiple outdoor study sites in Helsinki, Finland. On-site interviews were conducted 8–19 June 2021 between 2:30 pm and 8:15 pm in sunny or partly cloudy weather at around 20°C. Perceived greenery ratings were compared against several computational greenery measures derived from ground-level photographs, LiDAR/point clouds, and top-down remote sensing/land cover within buffers (50 m buffer found to best match perceived greenery).
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Torkko, J., Poom, A., Willberg, E., & Toivonen, T. (2023). How to best map greenery from a human perspective? Comparing computational measurements with human perception. Frontiers in Sustainable Cities, 5.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Data were collected in a smaller, managed institutional (campus) environment with observation points scattered across the study area. Morning and evening datasets were not balanced at every location. The framework uses mobile device orientation (alpha/beta/gamma) and GPS geolocation to filter and geotag images, and applies computer vision (e.g., Places365 scene classification, ADE20K labels, and Faster R-CNN object detection) to quantify visual/environmental attributes such as naturalness, complexity, familiarity, and openness. Results may differ in typical urban areas that are more dynamic and diverse in traffic and interrelated factors; lighting conditions can vary due to overshadowed pockets.
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: ADE20K training dataset; image quality sensitive to lighting/weather
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Verma, D., Jana, A., & Ramamritham, K. (2018). Quantifying Urban Surroundings Using Deep Learning Techniques: A New Proposal. Urban Science, 2(3), 78.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Applies to assessing neighbourhood greenspace quantity and street-level greenspace quality in Guangzhou, China using street view images sampled at many points (four headings per point) and remote-sensing greenspace (Landsat 8, 30 m). Neighbourhood socioeconomic conditions are based on Guangzhou census data. Street-view images are horizontal (vertical angle = 0) and represent streetscapes rather than only within-park environments.
applicability.spatial_context: Guangzhou, China; Guangzhou; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Wang, R., Feng, Z., Pearce, J., Yao, Y., Li, X., & Liu, Y. (2021). The distribution of greenspace quantity and quality and their association with neighbourhood socioeconomic conditions in Guangzhou, China: A new approach using deep learning method and street view images. Sustainable Cities and Society, 66, 102664.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Field study in Zhengzhou, China during winter (cold and dry season). Outdoor thermal comfort assessed via on-site microclimate monitoring (air temperature, relative humidity, wind speed, globe temperature) and questionnaire surveys. Urban greenery exposure quantified from two dimensions: horizontal greenery using Green View Index (GVI) from street-level photos and vertical canopy cover using Tree View Factor (TVF) from upward fisheye images processed in WinSCANOPY. Participants were outdoor users/volunteers surveyed at the sites during measurements; clothing insulation and metabolic rate estimated using ASHRAE 55 and ISO 7730.
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Wang, K., Fang, X., Ma, Y., Xue, S., & Yin, S. (2022). Assessing Effects of Urban Greenery on the Regulation Mechanism of Microclimate and Outdoor Thermal Comfort during Winter in China's Cold Region. Land, 11(9), 1442.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Field study in Zhengzhou, China during winter (cold and dry season). Outdoor thermal comfort assessed via on-site microclimate monitoring (air temperature, relative humidity, wind speed, globe temperature) and questionnaire surveys. Urban greenery exposure quantified from two dimensions: horizontal greenery using Green View Index (GVI) from street-level photos and vertical canopy cover using Tree View Factor (TVF) from upward fisheye images processed in WinSCANOPY.
