applicability.raw_conditions_text: Cross-sectional sidewalk study of Hong Kong (all 18 districts). Sentinel-2 (4 Jan 2022, 10 m, 13 bands) Sen2Cor-corrected, NDVI from Band 4/Band 8 in QGIS. Python crawler grabbed 18,000 Google Street View images (1,000/district; 640x400 px); DeepLabV3-ResNet101 trained on Cityscapes (5,000 imgs, 30 classes) gave GVI. Questionnaire (71 distributed, 48 valid; F:M 43:56) set weights (NDVI 0.17, GVI 0.09). Walking greening index = weighted normalized sum. Self-reported, small sample.
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Wang, H., & van Ameijde, J. (2023). Quantifying the Impact of Pedestrian-level Greening on the Perception of Walking. CAADRIA Proceedings, 2, 451–460.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Inner London, United Kingdom, analysed using crowdsourced Mapillary street-level imagery. The study built Integrated Visual Walkability (IVW) and Type of Visual Walkability (TVW) from four indicators - greenery, openness, visual pavement, and crowdedness of transportation - derived via panoptic segmentation, and examined their street-segment distribution and seasonal transitions.
applicability.spatial_context: Inner London, United Kingdom; street-segment level
applicability.temporal_scope: spring season; summer season; autumn season; winter season
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Mapillary (crowdsourced); panoptic segmentation; uneven image availability across segments.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily descriptive and observational spatiotemporal analysis; the paper does not establish causal intervention effects.

source.citation: Wang, M., Haworth, J., Chen, H., Liu, Y., & Shi, Z. (2024). Investigating the potential of crowdsourced street-level imagery in understanding the spatiotemporal dynamics of cities: A case study of walkability in Inner London. Cities, 153, 105243.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in central areas of Beijing (within Fifth Ring Road), Nanjing (main urban area), and Shanghai (within Outer Ring Road), China. A total of 687,354 summertime Baidu Street View images within 1–5 km radius of air quality monitoring sites were collected. Deep learning methods extracted the vertical distribution of street greening and buildings from street-view imagery. A green index and building index were proposed to quantify street-level vegetation and building presence. Summertime (May–October 2019) hourly air pollution data (AQI, NO₂, PM2.5, PM10) from 25 monitoring sites were used. Multilevel regression analysed associations between street green index, building index, and air pollution indicators.
applicability.spatial_context: Beijing; Fifth Ring Road; Outer Ring Road; 1–5 km radius of air quality monitoring; street-network level
applicability.temporal_scope: data period: 2019; summer season; spring season; autumn season
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Wu, D., Gong, J., Liang, J., Sun, J., & Zhang, G. (2020). Analyzing the Influence of Urban Street Greening and Street Buildings on Summertime Air Pollution Based on Street View Image Data. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 9(9), 500.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study focuses on residential courtyard microclimate and outdoor thermal comfort using simulated courtyard models calibrated/validated with field data from four historic Chinese gardens in Guangdong Province (Keyuan Garden, Yuyin Hill House, Qinghui Garden, Liangyuan Garden). Simulations compute mean radiant temperature (Tmrt) and wind speed (vair) on uniform grids at ~1.5 m height, and relate these to COMFAcourtyard/thermal discomfort using a data-driven statistical workflow. Weather scenarios vary air temperature (5, 15, 25, 35 C), cloud cover (1/5/9) and wind direction/speed (N/E/S/W; 0.5–3 m/s). Empirical validation uses 2014–2015 on-site thermal sensation votes (ASHRAE 9-point scale) from 1677 questionnaires at 11 points after excluding winter data.
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Wu, R., Fang, X., Brown, R., Liu, S., & Zhao, H. (2023). Establishing a link between complex courtyard spaces and thermal comfort: A major advancement in evidence-based design. Building and Environment, 245, 110852.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted across five districts of Nanjing, China. A novel integrated framework combined street greenery assessment with accessibility analysis to evaluate daily street-level greenery visible to residents. Advanced image semantic segmentation was applied to Baidu Street View images to compute Green View Index (GVI) at street sampling points. Accessibility to green spaces was measured using network analysis. The integrated model provides a new measurement methodology to quantify the quality of urban street greening, with results mapped and analysed at the district and neighbourhood scales.
applicability.spatial_context: five districts of Nanjing, China; Nanjing; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Wu, Z., Xu, K., Li, Y., Zhao, X., & Qian, Y. (2024). Application of an Integrated Model for Analyzing Street Greenery through Image Semantic Segmentation and Accessibility: A Case Study of Nanjing City. Forests, 15(3), 561.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Applies to high-density urban communities in Shanghai, China, examining associations between neighborhood greenness and residents' BMI/obesity. Greenness is measured via three features: green access (e.g., distance to nearest city park), green exposure (satellite NDVI within a 1,000 m community buffer), and eye-level street greenery using a View Green Index (VGI) derived from Baidu Street View images segmented with a deep convolutional neural network (SegNet). Outcomes are BMI classes (normal, overweight, obesity) modeled with multilevel mixed-effects ordered logistic regression controlling for sociodemographics (age, gender, marital status, education, income, job), community location, and self-rated health. Findings are intended for similar dense built environments where vertical/street-level greening is relevant to daily life and walking-scale exposure (about 1 km).
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; regression modelling; machine learning modelling
applicability.spatial_context: Shanghai, China; Shanghai; neighbourhood scale; street-network level
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Xiao, Y., Zhang, Y., Sun, Y., Tao, P., & Kuang, X. (2020). Does Green Space Really Matter for Residents' Obesity? A New Perspective From Baidu Street View. Frontiers in Public Health, 8.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Shanghai, China, examining the spatial distribution pattern of street greenery and its relationship with socioeconomic status and the built environment. The Green View Index (GVI) was used as the main evaluation indicator, computed from street-view imagery via semantic segmentation. Analysis was performed at both county (district) and community (subdistrict/jiedao) scales. Spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I) was used to identify clustering patterns of street greenery. Relationships between GVI and socioeconomic variables (e.g., housing price, population density, income level) as well as built-environment characteristics (e.g., road density, building density, land-use mix) were examined using regression analysis.
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Xiao, C., Shi, Q., & Gu, C.-J. (2021). Assessing the Spatial Distribution Pattern of Street Greenery and Its Relationship with Socioeconomic Status and the Built Environment in Shanghai, China. Land, 10(8), 871.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Field study in June in five urban parks in Changchun, China. Micro-climate meteorological factors were monitored and thermal comfort was evaluated, with additional assessment using facial expression emotion scores. Vegetation exposure was characterized using streetscape photos to extract Green View Index (canopy/lawn/grassland) via a DeepLabV3+ model, and plant species were checked by volunteers/professionals.
applicability.spatial_context: June in five urban parks in Changchun, China; Changchun
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban street-view imagery is segmented with the proposed PSAT-Net semantic segmentation model to extract vegetation and compute Green View Index (GVI). The study evaluates how different street-view camera parameters (e.g., pitch/elevation angle, field of view, and heading configurations) influence the resulting GVI values. It is intended for street-level urban scenes captured by vehicle-mounted, pedestrian-carried, or drone-based cameras, and is relevant where lighting conditions vary across capture times.
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Xu, H., Lu, H., & Liu, S. (2024). Online Street View-Based Approach for Sky View Factor Estimation: A Case Study of Nanjing, China. Applied Sciences, 14(5), 2133.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Eye-level street greenery assessment in Guangzhou, China using Baidu Map panoramic images (requested every 20 m, January 2020). Green View Index (GVI) computed as the proportion of vegetation pixels in four directional images per location point, with vegetation extracted via Visible Difference Vegetation Index (VDVI) thresholding. Results analyzed at point and neighbourhood scales with spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I) and hotspot analysis; correlations examined with land-use/road grade and housing attributes (house price, building age).
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 20 m; Baidu Street View
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

source.citation: Xue, C., Jin, C., Zhou, L., & Li, G. (2022). Exploring the distribution of city street greenery from eye-level: An application of Baidu Map panoramic images data. Geografisk Tidsskrift-Danish Journal of Geography, 122(1), 73–86.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Editorial overview of methods for measuring human-scale urban form and performance, including use of street-view imagery and machine learning. Mentions street canyon solar radiation estimation and a case example in the Hutong area of Beijing using Tencent Street View imagery (2012 and 2016) to assess visual quality and temporal variation of street space.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; longitudinal/temporal analysis; machine learning modelling
applicability.causal_limitations: Primarily cross-sectional/observational evidence; causal limitations were not explicitly discussed in source.

source.citation: Ye, Y., Zeng, W., Shen, Q., Zhang, X., & Lu, Y. (2019). The visual quality of streets: A human-centred continuous measurement based on machine learning algorithms and street view images. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science, 46(8), 1439–1457.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Systematic review of street view imagery (SVI) studies supporting Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Applicable to street-level environments where SVI is available; focuses on extracting streetscape element features (e.g., presence/absence and proportions of objects like greenery and built elements) and abstract deep-learning features to relate to SDG-related variables (notably SDG 11, SDG 3, SDG 13, SDG 2). Notes privacy/ethics considerations for capturing people on streets and that SVI may not cover SDGs far from streets (e.g., SDG 14).
applicability.causal_limitations: review paper; findings synthesised from multiple sources

source.citation: Yin, C., Peng, N., Li, Y., Shi, Y., Yang, S., & Jia, P. (2023). A review on street view observations in support of the sustainable development goals. International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation, 117, 103205.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Urban Hong Kong SAR context. Participants carried a GPS-equipped mobile phone for one week (5-second interval) and completed questionnaires and 2-day activity-travel diaries. Dynamic greenspace exposure was measured along GPS trajectories using top-down NDVI and eye-level street-view greenery (Google Street View images sampled every 50 m; PSPNet segmentation). Outcomes include self-reported mental health status (WHO-5) and momentary stress; cross-sectional design.
applicability.study_scope: Single-city case study; cross-sectional design; survey-based
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: sampling interval: 50 m; Google Street View; PSPNet segmentation
applicability.causal_limitations: cross-sectional (no temporal causality); self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

source.citation: Yu, C., & Kwan, M.-P. (2024). Dynamic greenspace exposure, individual mental health status and momentary stress level: A study using multiple greenspace measurements. Health & Place, 86, 103213.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Dalian, China (a second-tier aging city). Participants were 879 community-dwelling adults aged 60+ years. The study compares multiple objective greenspace exposure metrics (street view-, land use-, and satellite-derived; including NDVI, vegetation coverage, and park coverage) around residences using neighborhood boundaries and walkable buffers (e.g., 300 m). Outcomes are mental wellbeing (place-related emotional wellbeing, emotional wellbeing, evaluative wellbeing) from the WHO SAGE/WAVE 1 questionnaire. Mediation pathways tested via neighborhood environmental perceptions: perceived noise, aesthetics, and satisfaction with recreational opportunities.
applicability.population_scope: older adults (60+); survey/experiment participants; urban residents
applicability.causal_limitations: self-reported outcomes (potential reporting bias)

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in Hong Kong, a very high-density and compact city. Eye-level street greenery exposure was measured using Baidu Street View images sampled every 50 m along streets (four directions per point) and summarized as a Green View Index (0 to 1) within a buffer around participants' residences. Outcome focused on older adults' total walking time in six neighborhoods (n=180).

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Mobile on-road road surface temperature (RST) measurements along two street-canyon traverse routes (Route A and Route B) on 29 Aug 2018 in a dense urban area with high-rise buildings and street trees. Street-canyon morphology characterized using street-view imagery (semantic segmentation of panoramas to fisheye) to derive view factors (SVF, GVF, BVF) and sun exposure metrics (sunlit ratio, accumulated sunlit hours, screening status). Data aggregated by buffer radii and analyzed with Pearson correlations and multiple linear regression to predict daily maximum RST; reported strong positive SVF and negative GVF relationships with RST and large within-canyon side differences.
applicability.spatial_context: street canyons in hot/humid megacity (traverse routes)
applicability.causal_limitations: correlational/associational design

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted in the Lilong area in central Shanghai, China, using Baidu Street View images sampled about every 30 m along road centerlines. Visual street-space quality is evaluated from physical components extracted via deep-learning semantic segmentation and POI-based functional diversity within a 15 m buffer. Indicators include greenery, openness (sky ratio), enclosure (buildings/columns/trees), motorization, walkability (pedestrians and paving), and functional diversity; normalized and combined into a street visual quality index.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Study conducted along the Pearl River in Guangzhou, China, examining the relationship between visual perception of the urban riverfront core landscape area and the vitality of Riverfront Road. Landscape photographs captured by the research team along the riverfront were analysed using subjective environment perception prediction methods to quantify six perceptual dimensions. Vitality of Riverfront Road was evaluated through a multi-step process including measuring key visual indices (e.g., GVI, sky view factor, water view, enclosure, walkability) from street-view imagery, semantic segmentation, and relating visual perception scores to road vitality indicators.
applicability.causal_limitations: model-based prediction (not causal)

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Summer study in Fuzhou, China (hot and humid region). applicability.raw_conditions_text: Restorative quality of campus environments was assessed using machine-learning regression on street-view images (SVIs). Study site: Lihu campus of Jiangnan University (JNU), Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China (humid subtropical climate, ~32,219 students, 2166 km² campus area, 1.1 km² building footprint). Method pipeline: (1) extract visual features from SVIs via computer vision (pixel-level, semantic-level, scene-level — including depth-related features such as foreground depth mean DPT_MEAN); (2) collect PRS-11 restorative-quality labels (being-away, coherence, scope, fascination) via online survey; (3) train a gradient-boosted regression tree (GBDT) model predicting restorative quality from SVI features. Sample: 1088 SVIs from campus locations. R² = 0.726 for the prediction model. Adjusted R² = 0.504 for high-level visual features (trees, semantic classes) vs lower for low-level features. Causal scope: cross-sectional correlational; SVIs collected as static observations.

Global benchmarking context: the Place Pulse 2.0 dataset comprises street-view image pairs from 56 cities worldwide, used to train and evaluate the SegFormer-B5 + ConvNeXt-B + Random Forest model that predicts six subjective perceptions (beautiful, boring, depressing, lively, safe, wealthy). The reported descriptive ML performance (average ACC = 78.56%; per-perception ACC 0.770-0.807; MAE<0.598; R^2>0.638) is computed on this multi-city training/test corpus rather than on the Guangzhou case study, and therefore reflects a global, multi-climate urban training distribution.
applicability.study_scope: Multi-city / multi-country benchmark on Place Pulse 2.0
applicability.population_scope: image-based crowdsourced perception annotations (Place Pulse 2.0 raters)
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: Place Pulse 2.0 SVIs used as training/test data; no controlled deployment in real outdoor settings.
applicability.causal_limitations: Descriptive ML benchmark; no causal inference about perception or behaviour is supported.

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

applicability.raw_conditions_text: Tiexi District, Shenyang, China: a dense old urban (former heavy-industrial) district. The Building Green View Index (BGVI) is computed from multisource spatial data (Baidu Street View imagery, building footprints/heights, vegetation layers) using a viewshed algorithm; the analysis covers 1997-2020 and 800 sites. The setting is a high-density, low-greenery urban old-district context (max annual mean BGVI 14.08% in 2002; >90% of buildings have BGVI<35% in every study year).
applicability.spatial_context: Tiexi District, Shenyang; old urban / former industrial district; site-level (800 sampled buildings)
applicability.data_acquisition_constraints: BSV image availability constrained early years; building height and footprint data sourced from municipal records.
applicability.causal_limitations: Descriptive/cross-sectional; no causal inference about perception or behaviour.

source.citation: Qi, L., Hu, Y., Bu, R., Xiong, Z., Li, B., Zhang, C., Liu, H., & Li, C. (2024). Spatial-temporal patterns and influencing factors of the Building Green View Index: A new approach for quantifying 3D urban greenery visibility. Sustainable Cities and Society, 111, 105518.
